claim: Currier A and Currier B employ two structurally distinct cipher alphabets in which a core set of glyphs is shared but a subset of approximately 5-8 glyphs is exclusive or predominantly exclusive to each sub-corpus, producing measurable vocabulary intersection below 60% when controlling for word length.
evidence_for: Multiple high-confidence beliefs (0.943, 0.944, 0.971) support two structurally distinct cipher tables for A and B
Currier B has more than twice the word count of A (23,766 vs 11,022), making glyph-frequency profiles statistically robust enough to identify exclusive glyphs
If cipher tables differ, words of identical length across A and B should show lower-than-chance overlap in glyph composition
evidence_against: Two scribes encoding the same language with the same cipher but different handwriting habits could produce apparent glyph-frequency divergence without a structural cipher difference
Shared positional constraints {o,c,q,s,d} / {y,n,l,r,o} appear in both sub-corpora, constraining how distinct the tables can be
test: Extract the per-glyph frequency distribution for Currier A and Currier B separately; compute Jensen-Shannon divergence between the two distributions. Then for each word length L from 3 to 8, compute the Jaccard similarity of the word-type sets in A vs B at that length.
test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence between Currier A and B glyph distributions; per-length-bucket Jaccard similarity of word types
test_threshold: Confirm distinct cipher tables if JSD > 0.05 bits AND mean Jaccard similarity across length buckets < 0.35; deny if JSD < 0.02 bits and mean Jaccard > 0.55 (consistent with same cipher, different scribal hands only)

tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: Two truly distinct cipher alphabets would leave a detectable fingerprint in glyph-frequency divergence and in low vocabulary overlap at matched word lengths, since the same plaintext words would be enciphered differently. This test distinguishes cipher-table divergence from mere handwriting variation.
